coredata.dc:title: Carbon-dot/attapulgite composite sonocatalytic towards efficient indigo-containing wastewater degradation based on the cavitation mechanism

coredata.dc:description: Catalytic degradation is an effective method to treat indigo carmine (IC) in wastewater, while high-performance catalyst materials need to be explored. Herein, a facile “spot-heating” synthesis method was employed, whereby the carbon dots (CDs) sonosensitizer was loaded onto the surface of attapulgite (ATP) with ultrasonic cavitation effect, resulting in a composite sonosensitizer ec-CDs/ATP (where ec-CDs represent CDs prepared from ethanol (e) and citric acid (c)). ATP served as a good carrier promotes uniform dispersion of CDs. Benefiting from the large specific surface area, more active sites on the surface of ec-CDs/ATP were exposed, which promotes efficient degradation performance for IC, with a 20 % improvement in performance compared to the reported pure CDs or composite CDs photocatalysts (Bi2O3/dots, Cl-dots). The selective deactivation reactions and relative characterizations further verify that the catalytic process is compatible with the sonocatalytic mechanism originating from the fracture of surface oxygen-containing groups. Importantly, ec-CDs/ATP with a small mass of CDs loading achieves the same catalytic performance as a large mass of pure CDs. This research innovatively presents a simple synthesis method of a composite CDs sonosensitizer and explores its mechanism of degradation, thus pointing the way to the design of multifunctional sonocatalysts for the degradation of IC in wastewater.

a) XRD patterns of ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP. b) FTIR spectra of ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP. c) Raman spectra of ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP. d) O 1s XPS envelop of ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP. e) C 1s XPS envelop of ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP. f)- h) N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP respectively, and inset corresponding the SBET (including Sext and Smicro).

a) Sonocatalytic activity of ATP, e-CDs/ATP, ec-CDs/ATP and no sonosensitizer. b) Liquid chromatography of IC degradation product at the moment corresponding to the sampling time in ec-CDs/ATP system. c)-e) Different effects on sonocatalytic activity of ec-CDs/ATP: c) US power, d) amount, and e) pH.

a) Schematic of selective deactivation of hydroxyl and carboxylic groups on the surface of ec-CDs/ATP; b) Comparisons of sonocatalytic degradation performance of IC employing ec-CDs/ATP, BA/ec-CDs/ATP and BrPE/ec-CDs/ATP as sonosensitizer, respectively. c) Degradation efficiency of ec-CDs/ATP for IC before and after adding e−, ·O2 −, h+ and ·OH radical scavengers, respectively. DMPO spin-trapping ESR spectra for the detection of d) •OH and e) •O2 − in water and methanol, respectively. f) The recyclability of ec-CDs/ATP for IC degradation. g) The pH variation of ec-CDs/ATP solution with US irradiation time.

[16] f) Comparisons of IC degradation efficiency on ec-CDs/ATP (sonocatalysts) and the reported α-Bi2O3/C-dots and Cl-CDs (photocatalysts). [13,66].

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Catalytic degradation is an effective method to treat indigo carmine (IC) in wastewater, while high-performance catalyst materials need to be explored. Herein, a facile “spot-heating” synthesis method was employed, whereby the carbon dots (CDs) sonosensitizer was loaded onto the surface of attapulgite (ATP) with ultrasonic cavitation effect, resulting in a composite sonosensitizer ec-CDs/ATP (where ec-CDs represent CDs prepared from ethanol (e) and citric acid (c)). ATP served as a good carrier promotes uniform dispersion of CDs. Benefiting from the large specific surface area, more active sites on the surface of ec-CDs/ATP were exposed, which promotes efficient degradation performance for IC, with a 20 % improvement in performance compared to the reported pure CDs or composite CDs photocatalysts (Bi2O3/dots, Cl-dots). The selective deactivation reactions and relative characterizations further verify that the catalytic process is compatible with the sonocatalytic mechanism originating from the fracture of surface oxygen-containing groups. Importantly, ec-CDs/ATP with a small mass of CDs loading achieves the same catalytic performance as a large mass of pure CDs. This research innovatively presents a simple synthesis method of a composite CDs sonosensitizer and explores its mechanism of degradation, thus pointing the way to the design of multifunctional sonocatalysts for the degradation of IC in wastewater.

A composite sonocatalyst was synthesized by a facile “spot-heating” method for effectively degrading indigo carmine. Excellent degradation performance was achieved with a small amount of carbon dots loading, with 80 % degradation in 60 min. The degradation mechanism was verified by various characterizations. This provides a plausible explanation for the degradation by sonocatalysts and also guides the design of high-performance catalysts.
Unlabelled Image

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The treatment of organic pollutants in water resources was related to human health and ecological safety and has recently become a research focus. [1,2] Among them, indigo carmine (IC), a typical anionic dye, was widely applied as a colorant and additive in pharmaceuticals, textiles, and food industries. [3–5] While a large amount of unreacted IC inevitably flowed into natural water with industrial wastewater, causing serious water pollution and making it non-negligible organic pollution in wastewater. The traditional IC treatment methods represented by adsorption [6–9] and electrolysis [10,11] suffered from hard-recycle along with hazardous by-products, resulting in ineffective treatment of IC in application. While the emerging photocatalytic technology as well was struggling to achieve rapid and efficient degradation of IC due to its inefficient utilization of sunlight. [12–14] Therefore, an economical and efficient approach for IC degradation was still to be explored urgently.

Sonocatalytic, as an emerging promising water treatment technology for degrading various organic pollutants, has been investigated widely in recent years. Among the studies of sonocatalytic on water purification, reactive oxygen species (ROS) induced by ultrasound is believed to be a crucial intermediate in the realization of sonocatalytic reactions. In general, the hydroxyl radical (·OH) released by the pyrolysis of H2O or O2 under the extremely high local temperature and pressure, mediated reactions originate is the primary destruction pathway for organic pollutants. For example, Vidya Lekshmi et al. [15] verified the sonocatalytic degradation capacity of MnO2 for complete removal IC from water, and explained the essential role of high oxygen affinity in the sonocatalytic process. However, with the emergence of various sonocatalysts, including carbon-rich materials, metal compounds and their composites, the degradation process of organic matter in water has become more complex, and the specific mechanism of ROS generation under the action of ultrasound needs to be explored in depth. Recently, the discovery of carbon dots (CDs) with the sonocatalytic property for degrading rhodamine B (RhB) under ultrasonic (US), pointed to a new degradation mechanism and research ideas for the degradation of IC [16]. CDs nanoparticles were regarded as the new multifunctional catalytic materials based on the advantages of easy synthesis, inexpensive raw materials, unique optical properties, and high biocompatibility. [17–19] Moreover, the abundance of oxygen-containing functional groups on CDs surface, such as hydroxyl (-OH), carboxyl (-COOH), and amino (−NH2), afforded a design basis for self-modification or the construction of composite materials. [20,21] Currently, the synthesis routes for CDs consisted mainly of the “top-down” strategy obtained from carbon materials and the “bottom-up” approach gained from carbon precursors (such as organic small molecules). The former included arc discharge, [22] laser etching, [23] and electrochemical [24] methods, which combined a complex process with low throughput. The latter, involving microwave, [25] ultrasonication, [26] and hydrothermal [27] methods, were considered to be more efficient synthesis strategies. Among them, the ultrasonication technique occupied an important position in pure CDs or CDs composites synthesis for its advantages of low cost and simple operation. For instance, Xu et al. synthesized N-doped multicolored carbon dots with excellent chemical and optical stabilities only aided ultrasonic for 3 h. [28] And Zhang et al. successfully embedded highly crystalline carbon dots in 2D C3N4 nanosheets via the ultrasonic cavitation effect and demonstrated a positive connection between the number of CDs loaded and the ultrasonic conditions (including ultrasonic power and action time). [29]

The catalytic mechanism of CDs sonosensitizer originated from the cleavage of surface functional groups induced by the ultrasonic cavitation effect, [16] thus a carrier for supporting and dispersing CDs to expose more surface was essential. Furthermore, the presence of carriers facilitated the provision of more nucleation sites for cavitation bubbles, whose collapsing would deliver more energy for the breaking of chemical bonds. [30] However, few investigations have been conducted on suitable carrier materials for CDs, so it was urgent to find proper carrier materials for CDs-based composites and to further improve the sonocatalytic activity of CDs. Among the existing potential carrier materials, attapulgite (ATP), a natural nanorod-like mineral material, was considered an excellent carrier candidate for CDs. Its unique framework channels endowed the material with high specific surface area, ion exchange capacity, stable chemical properties, and good adsorption performance, which could effectively enhance the degradation efficiency of the catalysts. [31–33] In fact, ATP has already been adopted as a carrier material for various types of catalysts (photocatalysts, Fenton catalysts, et al.), which could efficiently improve the degradation performance while carrying and dispersing the catalysts. [34,35] For example, ATP-supported nano-Fe0 composite could be effective in preventing the agglomeration of nano-Fe0 and activating PMS to remove quinclorac from water. [36] Recently, Ma et al. [37] prepared a composite photocatalyst, ATP-Fe3O4-g-C3N4-PEI, with ATP as a carrier and grafted onto Fe3O4 and g-C3N4, which can be used efficiently for malachite green (MG) dye wastewater treatment. However, there was a lack of research on composites of ATP and CDs sonosensitizer, and the catalytic mechanism of the composite sonosensitizer remained undefined. Synthesis of composite sonosensitizers with reasonable structured and controllable cost and the further investigation of their catalytic mechanism could offer guidance and assistance in exploring novel composite sonosensitizer materials.

In this paper, we innovatively proposed a sonocatalytic degradation mechanism for generation ROS from bond-splitting of oxygen-containing groups. On this basis, we constructed a novel mineral-based composite material, which was demonstrated to prove its unique degradation mechanism and excellent IC degradation performance by various characterization means. Herein, ec-CDs/ATP loaded with CDs sonosensitizer was obtained by localized “spot-heating” method employing ATP as an immobilized carrier. And the composite sonosensitizer exhibited a favorable degradation effect on IC under ultrasonic irradiation. It required only a low loading of CDs to achieve degradation consistent with pure CDs. Meanwhile, the possible catalytic mechanism of the composited sonosensitizer was also verified. The results demonstrated that the catalytic activity of the composite primarily originated from the oxygen-containing functional groups, especially the -COOH groups, on the surface of loaded CDs. When a cavitation bubble collapsed, the oxygen-containing functional groups were broken, producing various reactants that reacted with the surrounding environment to generate ROS. It was worth noting that the catalytic activity was controlled by the solution pH. In an acidic environment, H+ induced more generation of ·OH and peroxyl radical (·O2 −). whereas in alkaline conditions, the ROS generation reaction was inhibited, which was expected to control the reactive concentration of ROS in the microenvironment. The above results indicated that the sonosensitizer with ATP as the CDs carrier was an excellent IC-degradation material. Among them, with enhanced sonocatalytic properties of CDs, ATP as a carrier promoted the dispersion of CDs sonosensitizer and provided more nucleation sites for cavitation bubbles. The combined effect of the two advantages improved the overall sonocatalytic performance, with IC degradation of over 80 % within 60 min. In this paper, we innovatively proposed a sonocatalytic degradation mechanism for generation ROS from bond-splitting of oxygen-containing groups. On this basis, we constructed a novel mineral-based composite material, which was demonstrated to prove its unique degradation mechanism and excellent IC degradation by various characterization means.

Attapulgite (ATP) was obtained from Rongwan Co., Gansu, China. Other chemicals such as critic acid monohydrate (CA), ethanol absolute (EA), indigo carmine (IC), benzoquinone (PBQ), Isopropyl alcohol (IPA), silver nitrate (AgNO3), ethylenediamine tetraacetic acid disodium (EDTA-2Na), carbon tetrachloride (CCl4), benzoic anhydride (BA), 2-bromophenylethane (BrBE) were purchased from Macklin Co., Shanghai, China and all chemical reagents above were utilized without further treatment. All aqueous solutions in the experiment were prepared using deionized water apart from liquid chromatography processing with ultrapure water.
Here, ec-CDs of ec-CDs/ATP represent CDs prepared from ethanol (e) and citric acid (c), while e-CDs of e-CDs/ATP represent CDs prepared from ethanol (e).

In the experiment, 0.5 g of CA was first mixed in 40 mL deionized water until dissolved, and then 1 g ATP and 60 mL EA were added sequentially to the above solution. After stirring at 10000 rpm for 24 h, the mixed solution was transferred to ultrasonic homogenizer (JY92-IIN, Scientz Co, Ningbo) with 130 W and thus subjected to a “spot-heating” induced by the ultrasonic cavitation effect for 30 min. Subsequently, the mixed solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 5 min and washed with ethanol absolute until neutral. Finally, the resulting deposit was dried fully in an oven at 60 °C to gain ec-CDs/ATP.
The preparation of e-CDs/ATP prohibited the addition of CA in the first step, and the following steps were performed exactly according to the above scheme.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selective deactivation of hydroxyl or carboxyl groups on ec-CDs/ATP surface
ce:section.ce:para: The experiment method of selective deactivation of -OH or -COOH groups was referred Ren et al. and was improved. Typically, 0.2 g of BA, or BrPE was dissolved in 10 mL CCl4. Subsequently, 20 mg of ec-CDs/ATP was added to the mixed solution. After stirring for 12 h, the mixed solution was allowed to dry naturally in a fume hood, and then the resulting solid was further washed with a small amount of CCl4 to remove physically adsorbed small molecules. Subsequently, the products were ultrasonically washed with ethanol absolute three times and dried completely in the oven at 60 °C. Finally, the product of ec-CDs/ATP grafted BA or BrPE molecules (namely BA/ec-CDs/ATP or BrPE/ec-CDs/ATP) was obtained, respectively.

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Zeiss G300) and higher solution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-F200) was employed to observe the morphology of samples. Preparation of the sample for the TEM investigation is presented as follows. Firstly, the powder sample was dispersed into ethanol and sonicated for 5 min, then the suspension was dripped onto a copper grid and dried by infrared lamp, and finally tested on the machine. The crystal structure of the samples was characterized by X-ray diffraction (XRD, EQUINOX 3000) using Cu Kα irradiation operating at 40 KV and 40 mA with a fixed slit. FTIR spectra were applied to record the spectra between 400 and 4000 cm−1 for analyzing the changes in the functional groups of the samples. Raman spectra of samples were performed with HORIBA Scientific LabRAM HR Evolution at an excitation wavelength of 532 nm. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha) experiments were performed and the binding energy was calibrated against the reference of C1s peak at 284.8 eV and O1s peak at 534 eV respectively. The Brunauer−Emmett−Teller (BET, ASAP 2460) specific surface area was determined using a Quantachrome Autosorb iQ instrument (N2 adsorption). High-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) was performed to detect the changes in residue in solution at different moments.

Sonocatalytic reactions were conducted by ultrasonic irradiation (US, KQ-100TDE) and the performance was evaluated using IC at an initial concentration of 10 mg L−1. Specifically, 10 mg of catalysts were mixed with 50 mL of solution containing the IC in a 150 mL beaker. Before irradiation, suspensions were mixed under vigorous stirring at room temperature for 30 min to achieve the adsorptive equilibrium. During the irradiation process, sonocatalytic water-splitting reactions were carried out in a circulating water-cooling system to maintain a relatively constant water temperature. After desired time intervals, the sample solution was taken in 5 mL and centrifuged twice at 4000 rpm for 3 min each time, and then the supernatant was separated to analyze the absorbance (Abs) of residual IC with a UV-6100 double beam spectrophotometer. Subsequently, the degradation efficiency of IC was calculated as follows:
Degradation efficiency =
A 0
−
A t
A 0
× 100 %
where A0 and At represent the initial Abs of IC and the corresponding Abs at t min reaction, respectively.

Here, a facile spot-heating method to prepare carbon dots coating ATP (ec-CDs/ATP) composites, as detailed described in the Experimental Section (Scheme 1 ). The original ATPs were partially dissociated in an aqueous ethanol solution under the effect of speed shear, and the solution with the optimal ethanol-water ratio effectively suppressed the aggregation tendency of these bundles, resulting in the formation of dispersed ATP bundles with a large contact area. It is then subjected to US carbonization in the specific solution. High-intensity US produces a cavitation effect, which generates a localized transient high temperature thereby causing small organic molecules (citric acid) in the solution to decompose, carbonize, and then deposit on the ATP surface in the form of CDs. At the same time, the cavitation effect will further dissociate ATP, exposing more active sites and promoting the uniform distribution of carbon sites on the ATP surface. And the untreated sample, the samples sonicated with a pure ethanol solution, and the sample treated with adding citric acid solution were denoted as ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP.

Morphology differences in ATP, e-CDs/ATP and ec-CDs/ATP were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM). The original ATP material displayed a rod-shaped microstructure arranged irregularly, with a smooth surface. And the rod was 30–50 nm in diameter and 0.1–1 μm in length which was highlighted by blue in Fig. 1a . Meanwhile this the sample showed obviously clustering phenomenon, where numerous short rods were closely packed together. As for the e-CDs/ATP (Fig. S1), the microstructure and surface were similar to that of the original ATP, except that the length of rob was diminished to below 500 nm. It indicated that the intense US in ethanol did not disrupt the essential structure of ATP or produce carbon dots on its surface. Nevertheless, the case of ec-CDs/ATP which was treated under the intense US in citric acid solution appeared distinct features, with numerous short robs arranged with low clustering, though it still retained the rod-shaped microstructure (Fig. 1b). Additionally, the significant rough particles generated on the ATP surface that were considered to be carbon dots formed from carbonization of citric acid. Moreover, the rod structure and surface morphology were further characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As shown in Fig. S2 and S3, the rod-shaped with a relatively smooth surface was observed in ATP and e-CDs/ATP samples. Whereas in the ec-CDs/ATP sample, some visible particles appeared on its surface (Fig. S4), which was consistent with the SEM image. To further confirm elements of particles, the component line scanning analysis was applied. The results of Fig. 1c provided sufficient evidence that the particles on the surface of the ec-CDs/ATP were carbon dots. As the scanning progressed, the carbon content presented a substantial enhancement at the particle points, whereas a uniform distribution across the surface of the ATP rod [38]. Furthermore, the line scans in ATP demonstrated a homogeneous distribution of carbon elements without a distinct elevated trend, confirming that successful deposition of carbon dots occurred only in ATP (Fig. 1d). In addition, the thermogravimetric analysis on ATP and ec-CDs/ATP also proved the result, with significantly higher weight loss of ec-CDs/ATP than that of ATP in Fig. S5. The above works illustrated that the preparation method of ec-CDs/ATP with intense US-induced could be successfully loaded with carbon dots while preserving the rod-shaped microstructure of ATP.

Then, the crystal structures of the above three samples were further investigated by X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis, which demonstrated that the three samples shared a similar pattern (Fig. 2a). It was obvious to notice that the characteristic peaks located at 8.47°,19.8°, and 34.9°, consistent with position of orthorhombic Palygorskite (PDF#082–1873) which attributed to (110) and (040) crystal planes, were presented in ATP and ec-CDs/ATP, and especially at 8.47° and 19.8° which nearly disappeared in e-CDs/ATP. [39,40] It suggested that the silicate backbone in ATP crystal was collapsed with the intense US in a single ethanol environment, whereas barely destroyed in a mixture of citric acid. Similarly, other peaks, muscovite (2θ = 8.84°, 17.76°, and 30.1°), [41] quartz (2θ = 20.86° and 26.64°), [42] and feldspar (2θ = 27.9°) [43] peaks presented in ATP, were better rendered in ec-CDs/ATP than e-CDs/ATP. The above phenomenon might be attributed that the protective and supporting effect of carbonization of citric acid on the ATP, attenuating the deformation of ATP crystal structure with intense US whilst preventing the collapse of crystal structure caused by the volatilization of ethanol during drying. It was worth noting that the crystal peaks of carbon in the ec-CDs/ATP curves were not observed, since the composite CDs were amorphous carbon without a complete crystal structure.

Thereby, the FTIR and Raman tests were performed to further characterize the chemical properties and carbon composition. As shown in Fig. 2b, the FTIR spectra of e-CDs/ATP and ec-CDs/ATP exhibited infrared absorption bands in close resemblance to those of ATP. All three featured the characteristic absorption band at 780 cm−1 and 977 cm−1 corresponding to Si-O-Si, [44] and 3618–3620 cm−1 belonging to the stretching vibrations of the (Mg/Al/Fe) OH within the ATP octahedra. [38,45] Except for the ec-CDs/ATP exhibited the marked enhancement of the C=C/C=O absorption band at 1620 cm−1, [20] which was attributed to the deposition of CDs on the ATP surface. The carbon composition information of the various samples was further unveiled by Raman spectra. In Fig. 2c, two typical peaks located around 1580 cm−1 (G-band) and 1360 cm−1 (D-band) [46] appeared for ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP, respectively. The G-band provided information on the ordered graphite structure formed in plane vibrations of sp2 bonded carbons, [47] and the D-band corresponded to sp3-defects in carbon, associated with disorder induced scattering. [48] In general, the ratio of peak intensities between the G-band and D-band, IG/ID, inflected the degree of graphitic, with a high value representing a high level of graphitization and crystallinity. [49] Calculating by the Raman curves, the initial ATP and e-CDs/ATP samples exhibited a similar value of IG/ID as 1.15 and 1.12 respectively, while the ec-CDs/ATP demonstrated the maximum value of 1.46. It was caused by the increased graphitic carbon content from the composite carbon dots. Furthermore, the peak of the D-band decreased by 29 cm−1 after treatment with intense US (e-CDs/ATP and ec-CDs/ATP), reflecting some disruption of the [-CO3] group in the ATP crystal structure. The above FTIR and Raman results illustrated that the carbon dots formed by the carbonization of citric acid successfully deposited on the ATP, and that the intense US did not induce any essential change in its chemical properties.

X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was performed to further determine the variation in the chemical environment of ATP before and after the composite carbon dots. The full recorded spectra showed all the samples presented similar elements, such as oxygen (O 1s), carbon (C 1s), silicon (Si 2p), magnesium (Mg 1s), aluminum (Al 2p), etc. (Fig. S6). Among them, the C and O could effectively reflect the composite properties between carbon dots and ATP, which were analyzed thoroughly. As shown in Fig. 2d, the C 1s spectrum in the samples was deconvoluted into three peaks at 284.64, 286.05, and 288.72 eV, respectively assigned to C=C/C-C, CO, and CO. [50] It should be noted that the positions of three peaks in e-CDs/ATP and ec-CDs/ATP were hardly shifted compared to the initial ATP, with only the relative content of three forms of chemical bonds of carbon. And the percentage changes could be visually exhibited in Fig. S7. In ec-CDs/ATP, the particular content of carbon present in the form of CO possessed a maximum value of 36.26 %, representing an increase of 6.85 % over initial ATP. It was contributed to the -COOH groups carried on the surface of the citric acid-derived carbon dots, and thus confirmed that the carbon dots successfully bound with ATP to generate ec-CDs/ATP. Whereas in the e-CDs/ATP, the change of carbon content occurred mainly in the chemical bond of carbon form as C=C/C-C, whose decrease originated from the disruption of the ATP structure by intense US. Meanwhile, the above explanation was supported by the variations of the lattice oxygen (OL) and adsorbed oxygen (OA) in Fig. 2e and Fig. S8. It was known that ATP had a natural layered-chain construction with an abundance of hydroxyl groups (OA-OH) and adsorbed water (OA-H2O) attached to the surface and pore interiors. [51] So, the O 1s spectrum was deconvoluted into three peaks at 530.41, 532.04, and 532.80 eV, respectively belonged to OL, OA-OH, and OA-H2O. [52] Carefully analysis of Fig. 2e in conjunction with the data in Fig. S8, the highest content of OA-H2O (64.01 %) and the lowest content of OA-OH (34.73 %) were explicitly exhibited in ATP. In contrast, the OA-H2O content of e-CDs/ATP was decreased, while the OL and OA-OH content were both slightly increased. This was possible that the OA-H2O was destructed available by the intense US and thus turned into free water, considering the OA-H2O bonded to the ATP with weak chemical interaction. Whereas, a significantly enhanced OA-OH content (66.61 %) could be noticed in ec-CDs/ATP, and the content of OA-H2O and OL were both lower than ATP. Most importantly, the OA-OH position was shifted towards higher binding energy. These implied that a significantly enhanced in OA-OH on ec-CDs/ATP surface as well as the emergence of novel adsorbed oxygen species, which was essentially contributed by the abundant oxygen-containing functional groups (h-OH and -COOH) on the carbon dots surface formed by citric acid derivation. The above characterization results fully demonstrated the successful generation of carbon dots, which provided abundant functional groups capable as the source of ROS.

To investigate the effect of carbon dots point on pore structure, the samples were further characterized by the N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms. For ATP, the curve belonged to Type-IV type isotherm with H3-type hysteresis (Fig. 2f). It coincided with the feature that classic ATP possessed narrow slit-like pores microstructure. [53] Meanwhile, the curves for e-CDs/ATP and ec-CDs/ATP similarly manifested typical adsorption hysteresis loops caused by capillary condensation phenomena (Fig. 2g and h). And the trend resembling ATP proved that the pore structure of the composite samples was not destroyed and still maintained the existence of mesopores. Additionally, from the inset data of the three graphs, the BET-specific surface area (SBET) of ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP, 180.12 m2 g−1, 185.44 m2 g−1, and 189.78 m2 g−1, respectively, showed an increasing trend. Among them, the external surface (Sext) and the microporous surface (Smicro) of origin ATP were 166.23 m2 g−1 and 13.90 m2 g−1, correspondingly. Compared to it, the increased SBET of e-CDs/ATP was primarily contributed by Smicro (17.22 m2 g−1), suggesting that intense US favored the dissociation of clustering ATP rods and thus exposed more micropores. And this was supported by the reduced overlap of the adsorption-desorption curves, as also evidenced by SEM results (Fig. S1). As for the ec-CDs/ATP sample, the SBET value reached a maximum of 189.78 m2 g−1, whereas the majority was contributed by the Sext (185.50 m2 g−1), with Smicro only accounting for 4.28 m2 g−1. The phenomenon was essentially related to the carbonization of citric acid on the ATP surface. [54] On the one hand, the carbon dots formed by carbonization were deposited on the surface of ATP rods and thus increasing in roughness brought additional adsorption sites. On the other hand, carbonization might also occur inside the pores of the ATP depending on the citric acid penetration. Both factors ultimately led to reduced pore sizes or blocked pores in ec-CDs/ATP, which explained the significant decrease in the calculated Smicro contribution. Based on the results above, the presence of carbon dots was supported by various characterizations, and the deposition on the ATP could improve the surface roughness without disturbing the chemical or crystal structure.

Based on the above results, it was reasoned that the complete structure of ATP supplied sufficient active sites for carbon dots loaded, while the carbon dots with numerous O-containing functional groups played a vital role in sonocatalytic activity. Therefore, IC, a typical organic pollutant, was chosen as the reaction substrate to investigate the sonocatalytic activity of ec-CDs/ATP. Fig. 3a illustrated the degradation performance of the blank sample and ATP, e-CDs/ATP, and ec-CDs/ATP samples. Within 60 min, the degradation trend of ATP on IC was essentially the same as that of the blank sample, suggesting that ATP had almost no adsorption or degradation effect on IC. In contrast, both e-CDs/ATP and ec-CDs/ATP exhibited a degrading trend for IC, demonstrating that they had a certain degradation performance. While the ec-CDs/ATP displayed pronounced degradation capacity than e-CDs/ATP, with over 80 % degradation efficiency reached by the former whereas the latter only achieved 28 %. In addition, we explored the degradation effect of the composites after different treatment times. It was obvious from Fig. S9 that the degradation efficiency increases with the enhancement of treatment time. These results suggested that the excellent degradation property was related to the carbon dots on the ATP, which generated ROS for effectively facilitating the decomposition of IC under US irradiance. To further assessed whether the color change in IC originated from adsorption or degradation, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) analysis was applied to reveal the degradation behavior of IC solution with the assistance of ec-CDs/ATP. From Fig. 3b, the initial curve (yellow one) of IC exhibited two clear peaks at 4.20 and 4.67 min. As the processing of the degradation, the peak at 4.20 min gradually disappeared accompanying a new peak appearing at 4.86 min. Where the disappearance represented the decomposition of IC and the emerging one contributed to the decomposed small molecule products. What was firmly supported by the above phenomena is that the effect of ec-CDs/ATP on IC was a degradation behavior rather than the absorption process. After confirming the degradation behavior of ec-CDs/ATP, the following step further determined the degradation efficiency to the effect of different factors, such as US power and sample amount. Afterward, the degradation efficiency of ec-CDs/ATP was investigated with IC as the primary dye. Fig. 3d indicated the degradation efficiency in the range of 0-100 W ultrasonic power (0 W test at 700 rpm stirring). As could be seen that the degradation capacity increased proportionally with increasing US power and reached a maximum level at 100 W. It was noticed that there was almost no degradation capacity at 0 W, indicating that the US was a necessary factor in the sonocatalytic activity with no ability to be replaced by the stirring. All this was explained by the correlation of the sonocatalytic activity with the ultrasound cavitation effect, where the extreme heat and pressure caused by the ruptured cavitation bubbles prompted the breaking of the chemical bonds on the carbon dots surface to generate ROS and thus degrade the organic matter. Moreover, the degradation kinetics under different US power were shown in Fig. S10. All of them complied with First-order kinetics which suggested high degradation efficiency. And the highest degradation rate constant reached 2.72 × 10−2 at 100 W, while 0 W hardly showed a degradation rate. Then, the influence of the added amount of ec-CDs/ATP on IC degradation was provided in Fig. 3d. It could be found that the increase in amount could slightly enhance the degradation efficiency as the increasing cavitation bubbles under US irradiation. And the small gap between the 30 mg and 40 mg curves implied that any further increase in the number of ec-CDs/ATP had a limited improvement in degradation performance. Finally, an inner factor, the solution pH, also influencing the degradation efficiency, was explored. Here, the degradation performance of ATP on IC in different pH environments was first explored. It was found that the pH of the solution did not change the adsorption performance of ATP (Fig. S11). As shown in Fig. 3e, among the pH conditions, the pH = 1 environment exhibited an optimum degradation performance, where the efficiency was up to 95 % within 60 min. Furthermore, the degradation capacity of IC was extremely higher in acidic conditions compared to that in alkaline conditions only with an efficiency of around 40 %. These results pointed to the profound influence of the acid/base environments on the sonocatalytic activity. The improved degradation performance of acidic conditions came from the increased activity species, including ·OH and ·O2 −. In fact, the primary oxidant produced during the sonocatalytic process was O radicals (·O−), originating from the breakage of O-functional groups on the sonosensitizer surface. [16] Based on the literature, [16,55,56] whether the ·O− played a role in the sonocatalytic reaction process was not known, but it could react with H+ to generate ·OH and ·O2 − based on reaction (1) and (2).

Similarly, the H+ could facilitate the interaction of ·O− and ·OH to further generate ·O2 − based on the reaction (3) and thus promoted the generation of higher reactive species under US irradiation.

While in alkaline environments, reaction of (1), (2), and (3) were inhibited and tended to undergo reversible (4).

This made it difficult to generate active species available to catalyze the reaction, thereby rendering the sonocatalytic activity inferior to an acidic environment. The detailed discussions on the sources and specific mechanisms of ·O− would be further processed in the following section.

The sonocatalytic activity of carbon dots was correlated with the fracture of oxygen-containing groups (-OH and -COOH) on their surfaces as demonstrated by Ren et al. Under US irradiation, [16] cavitation bubbles underwent rapid expansion followed by violent collapse, releasing heat up to 7700 K and pressure up to 81 MPa. [55] And the extreme temperatures and pressure generated during the collapse process were capable of breaking down the oxygen-containing groups on the surface of the sonosensitizers at the center of the bubble rupture and thus giving rise to various intermediate products, including oxygen radicals, hydrogen ions, and others. Subsequently, these intermediates reacted with the surrounding environment, resulting in the generation of highly active ROS. To verify whether the catalytic activity of ec-CDs/ATP originated from the bond breaking of oxygen-containing groups, two specific reagents, benzoic anhydride (BA) targeting -OH groups and 2-bromoacetophenone (BrPE) specifically reacting with -COOH groups, [57–59] were employed for modification and thus obtained two samples, BA/ec-CDs/ATP and BrPE/ec-CDs/ATP (Fig. 4a). And the degradation tests of IC performed under the same experimental conditions for the two aforementioned samples and ec-CDs/ATP were presented in Fig. 4b. In comparison to ec-CDs/ATP, which exhibited favorable degradation performance, the degradation efficiency of BrPE/ec-CDs/ATP was nearly zero, whereas the degradation extent was only slightly diminished in presence of BA/ec-CDs/ATP, indicating that the presence of -COOH groups and -OH groups was crucial for the catalytic activity of ec-CDs/ATP and -COOH groups appeared to be the primary contributor to the ec-CDs/ATP sonocatalytic activity. These results illustrated that ec-CDs/ATP possessed a high density of surface-attached -COOH groups and -OH groups, especially -COOH groups, which acted as sources for generating intermediate active species. In addition, the results implied that -OH groups involved in the sonocatalytic process were relegated to binding to carbon atoms, since the single ATP containing an abundance of -OH groups on its surface displayed no catalytic tendency towards IC, and even the adsorption effect was negligible (Fig. 3a).

Generally, ROS as activity species played an important role in redox reactions in catalysis. [60–62] To identify the variety of activity species from sonocatalytic reaction, p-benzoquinone (PBQ), isopropanol (IPA), silver nitrate (AgNO3), and ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid disodium salt (EDTA-2Na) were taken as scavengers to interact with ·O2 −, ·OH, electrons (e−) and holes (h+), respectively. [63–65] Among them, ·O2 − and ·OH could be converted from O2 and H2O by e− and h+ as in reaction (5) and (6), [56] or generated by ·O− and H+ based on reaction (1), (2), and (3), and then participating in the redox reactions in sonocatalytic.
(5)
e −
+
O 2
→ ∙
O 2 −
(6)
h +
+
H 2
O → ∙ OH

Fig. 4c displayed the variation of degradation efficiency in different scavengers and the best performance was possessed in the blank, which further demonstrated the color loss of IC solution was dependent on the degradation behavior arising from the redox reaction of ROS. It was noteworthy that the degradation performance of ec-CDs/ATP exhibited different levels in the present with each of the four scavengers, with the addition of PBQ or AgNO3 inhibiting degradation more effectively than IPA or EDTA-2Na. These phenomena suggested that four active species were involved in the degradation process. To further demonstrate the radical generation of ROS, EPR spectra with DMPO technique was carried out in degradation process. As seen in Fig. 4d and e, no signal of DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·O2 − was detected at 0 min. When exposed to the ultrasonic irradiation, the characteristic signals of DMPO-·OH and DMPO-·O2 − were clearly observed, proving that ·OH and ·O2 − were generated successfully with ultrasonic irradiation. All of these results suggested that ·OH and ·O2 − were involved in the degradation process. Among them, ·O2 −, including the products converted from e− and O, played a dominant role in the sonocatalytic process.

To further demonstrate the source of the sonocatalytic activity depending on the depletion of oxygen-containing groups, three repetitive degradation experiments were performed on ec-CDs/ATP. It was clear from Fig. 4f that the degradation effect of IC gradually diminished with the increase in the number of replicate experiments, which indicated that the sonocatalytic process was belong to irreversible catalytic reactions. Meanwhile, the variation of the pH value of the solution during sonication was also monitored in Fig. 4g. Within 60 min, the pH value of the solution gradually decreased to ≈4.50 compared with the initial mixed solution containing ec-CDs/ATP. It was explained by the H+ formation from the breaking down of the OH valence bond in O=C-O-H on the surface of ec-CDs/ATP.

The above irreversible catalytic results were further proved by XPS. Then the O and C elements of ec-CDs/ATP before and after sonication were characterized to reveal the depletion of oxygen-containing groups. As shown in Fig. 5a, the element O in ec-CDs/ATP had changed after 180 min of sonication. Based on the previous characterization results, the OA species on the surface of the ec-CDs/ATP changed and -COOH groups were added. Form Fig. 5a, it could be observed clearly that the peak position of the reacted OA-OH/COOH was shifted towards the direction of lower binding energy. It indicateed that the -COOH groups in the ec-CDs/ATP has been reduced and the relative percentage of -OH groups rebounded. Moreover, the content of OA-OH/COOH (Fig. 5c) decreased from 66.61 % to 39.79 %, and the contents of OA-H2O and OL increased from 32.49 % to 57.59 % and 0.91 % to 2.62 %, respectively, and the overall trend was similar to the oxygen fractionation peaks of ATP after a fine single intense sonication, and these fully indicated that the depletion of the oxygen-containing functional groups during sonication, especially the -COOH group, was the key to the source of sonocatalytic activity. Fig. 5b showed the analysis of the changes of carbon in the samples after 180 min of sonication. Again, the decrease in CO and CO content was attributed to the depletion of -COOH and -C-O-H on the sample surface. Careful analysis of the peak positions showed that the CO peak position slightly shifted towards higher binding energy, while the position of the CO peak remained almost unchanged, which was attributed to the fact that the location of the broken oxygen-containing groups occurred mainly at the carbon‑oxygen single bond, especially in the -COOH group. The above test experiments and characterizations have demonstrated that the sonocatalytic activity of ec-CDs/ATP depends on carbon dot particles with abundant oxygen-containing functional groups, especially -COOH groups.

Based on the comprehensive analysis of the conducted tests and characterization results, the mechanism underlying the sonocatalytic activity of ec-CDs/ATP could be elucidated, as depicted in Fig. 5d. The ATP component of ec-CDs/ATP, possessing a large specific surface area, functioned as a platform for the dispersion of carbon dots. Meanwhile, the advantageous properties of ATP contributed to the creation of more active sites for cavitation bubble formation, consequently reducing the cavitation threshold and facilitating the generation of a greater number of cavitation bubbles for sonocatalytic reactions under US irradiation. The carbon dots within ec-CDs/ATP served as essential materials, conferring the material with a novel sonocatalytic activity. When subjected to ultrasonic irradiation, ec-CDs/ATP, acting as a sonosensitizer, experienced extreme temperatures and pressures resulting from the collapse of cavitation bubbles, with ec-CDs/ATP located at the center of this bubble collapse. It led to the violent rupture of chemical bonds on the surface of ec-CDs/ATP, particularly the oxygen atoms bonded to carbon atoms, which ensued in the production of various free radicals, such as H+, ·O−, and ·OH. Moreover, water molecules in the vicinity of the central region of the cavitation bubble could undergo thermal decomposition upon the transient high heat, resulting in the generation of additional ·OH and ·H. More importantly, the sonocatalytic activity of ec-CDs/ATP could be effectively modulated by pH, which was helpful to realize the control of activity species yield in the microenvironment. In acid conditions, sufficiency ·O− would consume H+, H2O, or ·OH to generate ·O2 −, based on the reaction (1), (2), and (3), respectively, which dominated a sonocatalytic process with ·O2 −.

To further evaluate sonocatalytic activity after clarifying the sonocatalytic mechanism of ec-CDs/ATP, the reported materials of carbon dots sonosensitizer for the sonocatalytic activity, and the pure carbon dots or carbon dots composites about degrading IC were referenced [13,16,66]. As shown in Fig. 5e, the sonocatalytic efficiency of two sonosensitizer, ec-CDs/ATP of this work and CDs reported in the literature [16], in terms of US power, concentration, and pH were separately assessed. There was little difference in performance between the former two, whereas ec-CDs/ATP had a higher catalytic activity approaching 95 % at the respective optimal pH concentration. Importantly the mass of loaded CDs for the ec-CDs/ATP sample was significantly less than that of pure CDs, while the degradation performance was similar for both. This indicated that ATP made the CDs dispersed uniformly to enhance the utilization efficiency of CDs, which could effectively reduce the amount of carbon resource in the same degradation target. Therefore, the production of ec-CDs/ATP and their application in the field of degradation were more beneficial. To further exemplify the excellent degradation performance of the sample, Fig. 5f compared the performance of various catalysts for the degradation of IC. It was obvious that ec-CDs/ATP sonosensitizer exhibited the highest degradation efficiency in a short time, with up to 80 % degradation of IC within 60 min. Suggesting that ec-CDs/ATP sonosensitizer could rapidly generate more active species in a short time and then involve in the redox reactions compared with the photocatalysts containing carbon dots. The above comparisons indicated that ec-CDs/ATP as a novel composite sonosensitizer presented a promising future in the field of catalytic degradation.

In summary, a mineral composite sonosensitizer ec-CDs/ATP, for the IC degradation performance of over 80 %, was prepared by a simple spot-heating technique, and its sonocatalytic activity was verified in this study. Distinct from the mechanism of conventional piezoelectric materials under US irradiation, the key to generate of ROS primarily relied on the cleavage of oxygen-containing groups of sonosensitizer surface driven by the ultrasonic cavitation effect. And the sonocatalytic process was highly sensitive to the pH of the ambient liquid. Especially in the acidic environment, sufficient H+ promoted the reaction of ·O− to generate more ·OH and ·O2 −, thus enhancing the catalytic activity of sonosensitizer. Additionally, ATP as the carbon dots carrier offered more nucleation sites for the formation of cavitation bubbles compared to pure carbon dots sonosensitizer. When cavitation bubbles collapsed, more heat and pressure were released for breaking the chemical bonds, which was conducive to producing more activity species available for the reactions. Importantly, the sonocatalytic properties of equal masses of pure CDs and ec-CDs/ATP sonosensitizer were similar, while the mass of CDs loaded in ec-CDs/ATP was significantly smaller than that of pure CDs. Whereas, the undesirable reuseable performance could be recovered to some extent by recycling and repeating acid-leaching/ultrasonic on the catalyst. Conclusively, this work gave a simple preparation method for mineral composite carbon dot sonosensitizers, as well as offering a new guiding direction for treating with the IC in the wastewater.
